epigraph: We do not remember days, we remember moments. The richness of life lies in memories we have forgotten.

opening_paragraphs.text: Katarina woke before dawn and stood at the window of the farmhouse overlooking the floodplain. The Sava River ran silver-grey under a thin mist, and on the far bank the cornfields of Posavina stretched toward a line of poplars that marked the old boundary road.

She dressed quietly, careful not to disturb her grandmother asleep in the next room. The floorboards in the hallway were original — wide oak planks fitted by her great-grandfather in 1923, the year the house was built — and she knew which ones creaked.

Outside, the air smelled of wet grass and woodsmoke from the neighbour's chimney. A pair of white storks circled the bell tower of the church in Slavonski Brod, three kilometres to the south, riding a thermal that pulled the mist upward into a pale sky.

middle_paragraphs.text: The envelope had been tucked inside a 1971 edition of a Croatian-German dictionary that her grandmother kept on the top shelf of the bookcase. Katarina found it while searching for a box of old photographs her mother had asked about.
The handwriting was precise and small, the ink faded to the colour of milky tea. It was addressed to Marija Horvat — her grandmother's maiden name — and postmarked from Vienna, November 1968.

Katarina sat at the kitchen table and read the letter twice. It described a concert at the Musikverein, a walk along the Danube canal in early snow, and a decision — stated plainly in the final paragraph — not to return.

midpoint_quote: There are letters that change the shape of a family the way a river changes the shape of a valley — slowly, and only visible from a great distance.

closing_paragraphs.text: She folded the letter back into its envelope and replaced the dictionary on the shelf. Her grandmother was stirring in the next room — the familiar sound of the bedframe shifting, then the shuffle of slippers on tile.

Katarina stepped outside again and walked down to the riverbank. The mist had lifted and the Sava ran clear and fast, carrying branches and leaves from yesterday's storm upstream in the Slovenian hills.

She thought about the concert in Vienna, the snow on the canal, and the man who had decided not to come home. She thought about how a single paragraph, written in fading ink fifty-seven years ago, could rearrange everything she believed about the woman now making coffee in the next room.

chapter_closing_line: The storks had landed on the bell tower. The river kept moving.
